headings.text: Methane Rising: How the Permian Basin Became America's Invisible Catastrophe
Six Years of Major Methane Events
The Sealed Wells of McKenzie County Are Leaking Anyway
What Happens When the Aquifer Runs Out Before the Lease Does
The EPA Office With One Inspector and 4,200 Refineries
The County That Sued Its Own Sheriff
Roughneck Lung: The Diagnosis Nobody Logs
Who Actually Owns the Orphan Wells

paragraphs.text: For six years, satellite imagery has tracked invisible plumes drifting north from West Texas oil country. Federal regulators have logged thousands of violations and collected almost none of the fines. Inside the slow administrative collapse that left a 75,000-square-mile sacrifice zone hiding in plain sight.
By Marisol Vance & Theo Brennan · 28 min read · Filed from Pecos, Texas

North Dakota regulators certified 1,847 abandoned wells as "permanently plugged" between 2009 and 2019. A new aerial survey by the Dispatch's data team found measurable hydrocarbon emissions at 312 of those sites — and the operators that sealed them no longer legally exist.
By Renata Holm · 12 min

In Haskell County, Kansas, a fourth-generation wheat operation watched its irrigation table drop seventeen feet in a decade. They are not leaving. They are not staying. They are something in between, and the federal disaster lexicon has no word for them yet.
By Cal Whitford · 9 min

Region 6 covers Texas, Oklahoma, Louisiana, Arkansas, and New Mexico. A staffing memo obtained through FOIA confirms what insiders have whispered for two years: the field enforcement division has collapsed below quorum.
By Imani Park · 7 min
Eight incidents the Dispatch judges most consequential, drawn from satellite, EPA, and state-level disclosure logs between 2018 and 2024.

"We stopped pretending years ago that this was a regulated industry. What we have in West Texas is an open-air emissions experiment with people living inside it."
    — Dr. Eleanor Sazaki, former EPA Region 6 enforcement chief
An unprecedented civil action in Eddy County names the local enforcement office as complicit in a brine-disposal scheme.
By J. Cordero · 6 min
Pulmonologists in Midland describe a respiratory pattern they can no longer call coincidence — and an insurance system designed to deny it.
By S. Aldama · 11 min
Six layers of shell entities, three offshore registrars, and a single Wyoming P.O. box: tracing liability through the bankruptcy maze.
By T. Brennan · 14 min
A line-by-line read of the EPA's 2024 final rule with margin notes from four regulators who helped write — and dilute — it.
By R. Holm · 8 min
Independent investigative reporting on environmental collapse, industrial negligence, and climate displacement across the American interior. Reader-funded since 2019.
One letter, every Friday morning. Original reporting, no syndication, no advertising.
